name: Sierra del Carche Loop from Jumilla
name_original: Puerto de Jumilla a Yecla y vuelta por Sierra del Carche — circular desde Jumilla
description: Cycle 37.5 miles through Sierra del Carche Regional Park, gaining 1614 feet on this moderate route from Jumilla.
region_intro: Road cycling around Jumilla offers diverse landscapes, characterized by mountainous terrain, extensive vineyards, and historical sites within the Altiplano region of Murcia, Spain. The area features several mountain ranges, including the Sierra del Carche Regional Park, which provides challenging ascents and scenic routes. Limestone formations and deep ravines are common, alongside picturesque routes through wine-producing areas. This varied topography provides a range of road cycling experiences. Best road cycling routes around Jumilla The most popular road cycling route is Ascent to the Monastery of Santa Ana and Cieza — return from Jumilla, a 53.2 miles (85.6 km) trail that takes 3 hours 44 minutes to complete. This route features a moderate difficulty grade and passes by the historic Monastery of Santa Ana. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Puerto de Jumilla to Yecla and back through Sierra del Carche — circular from Jumilla, a moderate 37.5 miles (60.4 km) path. This route offers scenic views through the Sierra del Carche Regional Park. Local road cyclists also love the Climb to Sierra Salinas from Jumilla via Sierra del Carche, a 46.8 miles (75.3 km) trail leading through mountainous terrain and the Sierra del Carche, often completed in about 3 hours 39 minutes. Road cycling around Jumilla is defined by mountainous terrain, extensive vineyards, and historical sites. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to more challenging routes with significant elevation gains. The routes in Jumilla are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.75 stars from more than 3 reviews. More than 300 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Jumilla's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Jumilla?
A: Jumilla offers a wide selection of road cycling routes, with over 30 different options available. These routes cater to various skill levels, from easy rides to more challenging ascents.

Q: What is the general difficulty level of road cycling routes in Jumilla?
A: The road cycling routes in Jumilla offer a diverse range of difficulties. You'll find 5 easy routes, 24 moderate routes, and 4 difficult routes, ensuring options for all fitness levels, from beginners to experienced cyclists.

Q: Are there challenging climbs for experienced road cyclists in Jumilla?
A: Yes, Jumilla's mountainous terrain provides excellent challenges for experienced road cyclists. The region includes peaks like Madama in the Sierra del Carche Regional Park, reaching 1,372 meters, offering significant elevation gains. Routes such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1009128287/climb-to-sierra-salinas-from-jumilla-via-sierra-del-carche" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Climb to Sierra Salinas from Jumilla via Sierra del Carche&lt;/a&gt; feature substantial ascents.

Q: Are there easier road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Jumilla?
A: Absolutely. Jumilla has 5 designated easy road cycling routes, perfect for beginners or those looking for a more relaxed ride. These routes typically feature less elevation gain and shorter distances, allowing for a comfortable introduction to road cycling in the region.

Q: What kind of scenery can I expect on road cycling routes in Jumilla?
A: Road cycling in Jumilla offers incredibly varied scenery. You'll ride through extensive vineyards, picturesque valleys, and mountainous landscapes, including the dramatic Sierra del Carche Regional Park with its unique geological formations. Many routes also pass by historical sites, adding cultural interest to your ride.

Q: Are there historical landmarks or points of interest along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, many routes incorporate historical and natural landmarks. You might pass by the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5052852" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monasterio de Santa Ana&lt;/a&gt;, or explore areas near the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4095975" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Carche Summit and Mountain Refuge&lt;/a&gt;. The region also features ancient sites like the 17th-century Snow Well in Sierra del Carche and even Bronze Age archaeological sites.

Q: Can I combine road cycling with wine tourism in Jumilla?
A: Jumilla is renowned for its wine production, and cycling through its extensive vineyards is a highlight. Many routes traverse the wine-growing areas, particularly around El Carche, offering a perfect opportunity to combine road cycling with wine tourism. For more information on the wine route, you can visit &lt;a href="https://www.spain.info/en/oenology/wine-route-jumilla/" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;spain.info&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Jumilla?
A: The spring and autumn months generally offer the most pleasant conditions for road cycling in Jumilla, with milder temperatures and less extreme weather. Summers can be quite hot, especially in the middle of the day, while winters are cooler but often still suitable for riding.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes in Jumilla?
A: Yes, there are several excellent circular road cycling routes in Jumilla. A popular option is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1009681531/puerto-de-jumilla-to-yecla-and-back-through-sierra-del-carche-circular-from-jumilla" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Jumilla to Yecla and back through Sierra del Carche — circular from Jumilla&lt;/a&gt;, which offers scenic views through the regional park and brings you back to your starting point.

Q: What do other cyclists say about road cycling in Jumilla?
A: The road cycling routes in Jumilla are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.75 stars. Cyclists often praise the diverse terrain, the beautiful vineyards, and the challenging yet rewarding climbs, making it a popular destination for over 300 road cyclists who have explored the area with komoot.

Q: Are there family-friendly road cycling options in Jumilla?
A: While many routes involve some elevation, there are easier options suitable for families or those seeking a less strenuous ride. Look for the 5 routes categorized as 'easy' in the guide, which typically feature flatter sections and shorter distances, making them more accessible for a family outing.

Q: Is parking available for cyclists near the routes in Jumilla?
A: Parking is generally available in and around Jumilla, especially in the town center and at various access points to the regional parks and natural areas. Many routes start from convenient locations where you can park your vehicle before heading out on your ride.
tips: Close to the town of Fuente del Pino we find ourselves rolling between cultivated fields and the surrounding mountains, such as Cingla, which give us a beautiful picture.
A beautiful picture that we found rolling near the Port of Jumilla and between the Sierra de la Magdalena and the Cerro de la Flor, between the towns of Yecla and Jumilla.
While we roll along the quiet road about 10km from Jumila. We are among cultivated fields on a well paved road with beautiful views of the Sierra del Carche.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: While the route is circular from Jumilla, specific parking details are not provided. It's recommended to find suitable parking within Jumilla itself, as the route begins and ends there, offering convenient access to the start of your ride.

faqs.question: How difficult is this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as moderate for road cycling. It's approximately 60.4 km long with an elevation gain of about 490 meters. While most surfaces are well-paved, some segments might be unpaved and challenging, potentially requiring good physical fitness and even pushing your bike. You'll encounter significant climbs and descents, making it a demanding but rewarding ride.

faqs.question: Is this a circular cycling route?
faqs.answer: Yes, this is a circular route that starts and ends in Jumilla, taking you through the Sierra del Carche and towards Yecla before returning.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is diverse, primarily navigating the mountainous Sierra del Carche Regional Park. Expect mostly well-paved roads, but be prepared for some potentially unpaved segments. The route involves significant climbs and descents, with altitudes ranging from around 541 meters to nearly 1,000 meters, offering a varied and challenging experience.

faqs.question: What kind of flora and fauna can I expect to see in Sierra del Carche?
faqs.answer: You'll experience rich Mediterranean flora, including Aleppo and maritime pines, holm oaks, junipers, strawberry trees, esparto grass, and lavender. The park is home to diverse fauna, with birds of prey like golden and booted eagles, eagle owls, and peregrine falcons, as well as foxes, badgers, and wild boars in the undergrowth.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The trail research suggests the region has a Mediterranean climate. For road cycling, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant temperatures, avoiding the intense heat of summer and potential colder, wetter conditions in winter. Always check local weather forecasts before you go.

faqs.question: What can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: The route offers dramatic scenery and several notable points of interest. You'll get a View of Sierra de la Cingla, pass through Puerto de Jumilla, and enjoy a View of Sierra del Carche. The landscape features picturesque ravines, dense pine forests, impressive rocky escarpments, and views of vineyards and salt mines.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route passes through natural areas, specific regulations regarding dogs on this particular cycling route are not detailed. Generally, dogs are allowed in many natural parks in Spain, but often require leashes. It's best to check with local authorities or the Sierra del Carche Regional Park administration for their specific pet policies.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this route through Parque Regional de la Sierra del Carche?
faqs.answer: No, typically there are no entrance fees or permits required for cycling through the Parque Regional de la Sierra del Carche. However, always respect park regulations, especially concerning protected areas, wildlife, and fire prevention.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly intersects with several other paths. You'll cross sections of the Camino del Altiplano, Transmurciana 25, Cañada Real de Murcia a Albacete, Camino de los Picarios, and Pista del Serral.

faqs.question: Are there any local specialties or unique features to look out for?
faqs.answer: Beyond the natural beauty, the region is renowned for its wine production, forming part of the Jumilla Wine Route, and its olive oil. The Sierra del Carche also has geological formations like the Cabezo de la Rosa saline diapir, and historical elements including evidence of human habitation dating back 5,000 years.

waypoints.name: View of Sierra de la Cingla
